title: Roast turkey
subtitle: Herb butter roasted turkey with crispy skin and juicy meat.
excerpt: Whole turkey rubbed all over with herb butter, stuffed with lemon and onion, roasted until the skin is golden and the juices run clear. Rested properly before carving. The Christmas centrepiece that needs no more than time and a good thermometer.
sourceNotes: Turkey was established as the standard British Christmas roast by the nineteenth century, having displaced goose in middle-class households where the bird was more affordable than beef or venison. The tradition arrived from the Americas: turkeys were brought to Europe in the sixteenth century and were adopted quickly as a festive bird because of their size. The herb butter technique is a modern refinement that addresses the bird's tendency to dry out, particularly the breast meat, by inserting fat between the skin and flesh.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the herb butter up to two days ahead and keep covered in the fridge. Bring the turkey to room temperature for an hour before roasting.

content.text: Turkey breast dries out before the legs are cooked through. Herb butter pushed under the skin of the breast slows this process: fat conducts heat more gently than air and the butter bastes the meat from inside. Starting breast-side down and flipping halfway through is an alternative approach; the butter method requires less vigilance.

The only reliable way to know a turkey is cooked is a thermometer. The thickest part of the thigh should read 75 °C. Resting for at least 30 minutes is not optional: the juices redistribute and the meat relaxes, making it far easier to carve.
A large roasting tin and a meat thermometer.
Make the herb butter and prepare the turkey

Heat the oven to 190 °C fan. Mix {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter with the chopped thyme, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Loosen the skin over the turkey breast by carefully sliding your hand between the skin and flesh from the neck end. Push most of the herb butter under the skin and spread it over both breasts. Rub the remainder over the outside of the bird. Stuff the cavity with the lemon, onion, and bay leaves.

Place the turkey breast-side up in a roasting tin. Cover loosely with foil. Roast at 190 °C fan for 20 minutes per kg plus 70 minutes (for a 4.5 kg bird, about 3 hours). Remove the foil for the last 45 minutes to brown the skin. The turkey is done when a thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the thigh reads 75 °C.

Transfer the turkey to a board, tent loosely with foil, and rest for at least 30 minutes before carving. Pour the resting juices into the roasting tin for gravy. Carve the breast in slices and remove the legs to separate the drumstick from the thigh.

items.cause: Overcooked, or insufficient butter under the skin.
items.fix: Check the temperature earlier next time: 75 °C in the thigh means the breast is done. Serve with plenty of gravy.

items.cause: Foil left on too long, or the bird was wet when it went into the oven.
items.fix: Pat the bird dry before buttering. Remove the foil at least 45 minutes before the end.
Return to the oven and check every 15 minutes. A thermometer reading of 75 °C in the thigh is the only reliable test.

items.symptom: Bird is browning too fast before cooked through
items.cause: Oven temperature high or skin is thin.
items.fix: Cover the breast loosely with a double layer of foil and continue roasting until temperature is reached.

content.text: The British Christmas roast turkey has American origins: Europeans had no turkeys before the sixteenth century, when Spanish explorers brought them back from Mexico. They spread quickly through European farming as a large bird that could be fattened efficiently. By the Victorian period, roast turkey was the aspirational Christmas centrepiece in Britain, replacing goose for the middle classes who could afford a bird large enough to feed the household. It has remained the standard ever since, annual controversies about its dryness notwithstanding.
